strategies.name: Pointer to Integer Truncation
strategies.target: A loop with a pointer induction variable and an integer induction variable, where the integer induction variable's type is smaller than the pointer size. The loop exit block should have a PHI node for the pointer.
strategies.rationale: The patch replaces `ptrtoaddr` with `ptrtoint`. `ptrtoint` can truncate the pointer if the target integer type is smaller than the pointer size. If `STy` (the type of the SCEV expression `S`) is smaller than the pointer size, `SE.getPtrToIntExpr(ExitSCEV, STy)` will create a truncation. Later, `DiffV->getType()` will be `STy`. `Builder.CreatePtrToInt(BaseV, DiffV->getType())` will also truncate `BaseV`. This might lead to incorrect offset calculations or assertion failures if the types don't match exactly as expected by subsequent operations.
strategies.expected_issue: The compiler might crash with an assertion failure or generate incorrect code because `ptrtoint` might truncate the pointer value if the integer type is smaller than the pointer size, whereas `ptrtoaddr` extracts the address part. If the integer type is smaller than the pointer size, `ptrtoint` will truncate, which might not be what's expected when calculating the difference.

strategies.name: Pointer to Integer Zero Extension
strategies.target: A loop with a pointer induction variable and an integer induction variable, where the integer induction variable's type is larger than the pointer size. The loop exit block should have a PHI node for the pointer.
strategies.rationale: Similar to truncation, if `STy` is larger than the pointer size, `ptrtoint` will zero-extend the pointer. This might lead to incorrect offset calculations or assertion failures.
strategies.expected_issue: The compiler might crash with an assertion failure or generate incorrect code because `ptrtoint` might zero-extend the pointer value if the integer type is larger than the pointer size.

strategies.name: Provenance Capture
strategies.target: A loop with a pointer induction variable and an integer induction variable. The loop exit block should have a PHI node for the pointer. The test should check if the provenance is captured and if it affects subsequent optimizations.
strategies.rationale: The patch replaces `ptrtoaddr` with `ptrtoint`. `ptrtoint` captures the provenance of the pointer, while `ptrtoaddr` does not. This might affect alias analysis or other optimizations that rely on provenance. While this might not directly cause a crash in `tryToReuseLCSSAPhi`, it could lead to miscompilations later on.
strategies.expected_issue: The compiler might crash with an assertion failure or generate incorrect code because `ptrtoint` captures the provenance of the pointer, while `ptrtoaddr` does not. This might affect alias analysis or other optimizations that rely on provenance.

strategies.name: Non-Integer Difference
strategies.target: A loop with a pointer induction variable and an integer induction variable, where `ExitSCEV` cannot be computed or converted to an integer type.
strategies.rationale: The patch removes the check `if (!Diff)` after attempting to calculate the difference. If `ExitSCEV` is not successfully converted to an integer type (e.g., if `isa<SCEVCouldNotCompute>(ExitSCEV)` is true), `Diff` might not be of integer type. The assertion `assert(Diff->getType()->isIntegerTy() && "difference must be of integer type");` might fail.
strategies.expected_issue: The compiler might crash with an assertion failure because `Diff` might not be of integer type if `ExitSCEV` is not successfully converted to an integer type.

strategies.name: Base Value Conversion
strategies.target: A loop with a pointer induction variable and an integer induction variable. The loop exit block should have a PHI node for the pointer. The test should check if `BaseV` is correctly converted to an integer type.
strategies.rationale: The patch replaces `BaseV = Builder.CreatePtrToAddr(BaseV);` with `BaseV = Builder.CreatePtrToInt(BaseV, DiffV->getType());`. If `DiffV->getType()` is not the correct address type, this might lead to incorrect code or assertion failures.
strategies.expected_issue: The compiler might crash with an assertion failure or generate incorrect code because `BaseV` might be converted to an integer type using `ptrtoint` instead of `ptrtoaddr`.

reason_thou:
Fix Understanding:
The patch reverts a previous change that used `SCEVPtrToAddrExpr` instead of `SCEVPtrToIntExpr` in `tryToReuseLCSSAPhi`.
The original code (before the revert) preferred `ptrtoaddr` over `ptrtoint` when comparing an integer SCEV (`S`) with a pointer PHI node (`ExitSCEV`).
The reverted code (after the patch) unconditionally uses `ptrtoint` to convert the pointer PHI node to the integer type of `S`.
It also changes the IR generation: instead of `Builder.CreatePtrToAddr(BaseV)`, it uses `Builder.CreatePtrToInt(BaseV, DiffV->getType())`.

Formal Semantic Modeling:
Before the fix (with `ptrtoaddr`):
When `STy` is integer and `PhiTy` is pointer, it tries to use `ptrtoaddr` to convert `ExitSCEV` to an integer. If that fails, it falls back to `ptrtoint`.
When generating IR, if `PhiTy` is pointer and `STy` is integer, it uses `ptrtoaddr` to convert `BaseV` to an integer.

After the fix (reverted to `ptrtoint`):
When `STy` is integer and `PhiTy` is pointer, it unconditionally uses `ptrtoint` to convert `ExitSCEV` to `STy`.
When generating IR, if `PhiTy` is pointer and `STy` is integer, it uses `ptrtoint` to convert `BaseV` to `DiffV->getType()`.

Assumptions Identified:
The reverted code assumes that `ptrtoint` is always safe and correct to use when comparing an integer SCEV with a pointer PHI node.
It assumes that `DiffV->getType()` is the correct type to cast `BaseV` to using `ptrtoint`.

Potential Cases to Break Assumptions:
1. Truncation/Extension: If `STy` (the type of `S`) is different from the pointer size, `ptrtoint` will truncate or zero-extend the pointer. This might not be semantically equivalent to `ptrtoaddr`, which extracts the address part.
2. Provenance: `ptrtoint` captures provenance, while `ptrtoaddr` does not. This might affect subsequent optimizations.
3. Non-integral pointers: For non-integral pointers, `ptrtoint` might be invalid or have different semantics.

Test Strategies:
1. Create a loop with an integer IV and a pointer IV. The integer IV should have a type smaller than the pointer size (e.g., i32 on a 64-bit system). The loop exit block should have a PHI node for the pointer IV. We want to trigger `tryToReuseLCSSAPhi` for the integer IV, comparing it with the pointer PHI node.
2. Create a loop with an integer IV and a pointer IV. The integer IV should have a type larger than the pointer size (e.g., i128 on a 64-bit system).
3. Create a loop with an integer IV and a pointer IV in a non-integral address space.

  %ptr.iv.1.next = getelementptr i8, ptr %ptr.iv.1, i64 1
  br label %loop.1.header

middle:                                           ; preds = %loop.1.header
  %cmp = icmp eq i8 %x, 0
  %cmp.ext = zext i1 %cmp to i64
  %gep.start = getelementptr i8, ptr %ptr.iv.1, i64 %cmp.ext
  br label %loop.2.header

loop.2.header:                                    ; preds = %loop.2.latch, %middle
  %ptr.iv.2 = phi ptr [ %gep.start, %middle ], [ %ptr.iv.2.next, %loop.2.latch ]
  switch i8 %x, label %loop.2.latch [
    i8 1, label %loop.3
    i8 4, label %loop.3
  ]

loop.2.latch:                                     ; preds = %loop.2.header
  %ptr.iv.2.next = getelementptr i8, ptr %ptr.iv.2, i64 1
  br label %loop.2.header

loop.3:                                           ; preds = %loop.3, %loop.2.header, %loop.2.header
  %iv.3 = phi i64 [ 1, %loop.2.header ], [ 1, %loop.2.header ], [ %iv.3.next, %loop.3 ]

  %ec = icmp eq i8 %0, 0
  %iv.3.next = add i64 %iv.3, 1
  br i1 %ec, label %exit, label %loop.3

exit:                                             ; preds = %loop.3
  ret void
}

Wait, the non-integer difference issue is about `Diff` not being an integer type. `Diff` is `SE.getMinusSCEV(S, ExitSCEV)`. `S` is an integer SCEV, `ExitSCEV` is a pointer SCEV converted to integer using `ptrtoint`. If `ExitSCEV` is not successfully converted to integer, it might be `SCEVCouldNotCompute`. But there is a check `if (isa<SCEVCouldNotCompute>(ExitSCEV)) continue;`. So `ExitSCEV` will be an integer SCEV. `Diff` will be an integer SCEV. So `Diff->getType()->isIntegerTy()` will always be true. The assertion `assert(Diff->getType()->isIntegerTy() && "difference must be of integer type");` will not fail.

If `PhiTy` is a pointer type, `STy->isIntegerTy() && PhiTy->isPointerTy()` is false.
Then `S->getType() != PN.getType()` is checked. If they are different pointer types, it continues.
If they are the same pointer type, `ExitSCEV` is not converted to integer.
Then `Diff = SE.getMinusSCEV(S, ExitSCEV)`.
`SE.getMinusSCEV` on two pointer SCEVs returns an integer SCEV representing the difference.
So `Diff` will be an integer SCEV.

Then it returns `Builder.CreatePtrAdd(BaseV, DiffV)`.

define void @scev_expand_ptrtoint(i8 %x, ptr %start) {
entry:
  br label %loop.1.header

loop.1.header:                                    ; preds = %loop.1.latch, %entry
  %ptr.iv.1 = phi ptr [ %start, %entry ], [ %ptr.iv.1.next, %loop.1.latch ]
  %c = icmp ule i8 %x, 1
  br i1 %c, label %loop.1.latch, label %middle

loop.1.latch:                                     ; preds = %loop.1.header
  %ptr.iv.1.next = getelementptr i8, ptr %ptr.iv.1, i64 1
  br label %loop.1.header

middle:                                           ; preds = %loop.1.header
  %cmp = icmp eq i8 %x, 0
  %cmp.ext = zext i1 %cmp to i64
  %gep.start = getelementptr i8, ptr %ptr.iv.1, i64 %cmp.ext
